cards.title: Start here: n is the foundation
cards.body:
The lowercase n establishes the core DNA of your font: stem weight, arch shape, and the fundamental spacing unit. Take your time here — every other letter will be judged against n.

The n gives you m, h, u, r, and i almost for free. The quality you put into n multiplies across the entire character set.
tips: Draw the left stem first — it sets stem width for the whole font
The arch should emerge naturally from calligraphic movement, not be constructed mechanically
Space the n alongside itself (nnn) before moving to other letters
The counter (white interior) width sets your spacing rhythm
sources: Design with FontForge — Creating o and n

cards.title: Draw s early — it's the hardest letter
cards.body:
The s is notoriously difficult to get right. Its spine angle, terminal style, weight distribution, and optical balance all interact in complex ways. Start s early in your process so you have time to iterate.

Critically: your s terminals will predict the terminal style of a, c, f, j, and y. The decisions you make here ripple through the whole font.
tips: The spine (central diagonal) should align with your overall stress axis
The two counters (white spaces inside s) should appear roughly equal in area — not equal in size
Upper counter is typically slightly smaller than lower to look balanced
Test at small sizes constantly — s problems are invisible at 200% zoom
sources: Design with FontForge — Creating Your Type DNA

cards.title: H and O: your capital foundation
cards.body:
Just as lowercase starts with n and o, capitals begin with H and O. These two letters define the relationship between your lowercase and uppercase — a critical proportion decision.

H establishes: vertical stroke weight, cap height, and the structural DNA for B, D, E, F, I, J, K, L, P, R, T, U.
tips: Capital stroke weights are typically 5–15% heavier than lowercase to appear equal
The crossbar of H must be optically thinned (see OPT-001)
H width sets the reference for N (slightly wider) and other wide caps
Establish cap-to-x-height ratio now — changing it later is painful
